text: The patient is positioned supine with the left arm elevated above the head. A neural integrity monitoring endotracheal tube and an orogastric tube are visible. The surgeon identifies the sternal notch and cricoid process as landmarks, tracing a line to the axilla to mark the incision site.
The surgeon makes an incision down to the pectoralis fascia and creates a flap above it, extending towards the clavicle. Lighted retractors are inserted to enhance visualization of the surgical area.

The surgeon continues dissection into the platysma, identifying the sternocleidomastoid muscle. A plane is created between the sternal and clavicular heads of the sternocleidomastoid, with the sternal head retracted anteriorly.
The surgeon exposes the omohyoid muscle, which may be divided for additional exposure. A dissection plane is carefully created beneath the strap muscles, avoiding injury to the internal jugular vein.

The surgeon performs further dissection under the strap muscles, exposing the thyroid gland. The dissection extends to the contralateral side to create space for robotic-assisted surgery. A mounted retractor is positioned beneath the strap muscles to facilitate robot docking.
The surgeon stimulates the vagus nerve to confirm neural integrity before proceeding with the dissection. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is identified and stimulated, ensuring its functionality.

The surgeon separates the thymus from the thyroid gland, opens the carotid sheath, and identifies the vagus nerve for placement of a continuous neural monitoring clip. The thymus is carefully dissected off the trachea, ensuring no injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The surgeon retracts the thymus cephalad and delivers it from the thorax, carefully dissecting it from the innominate vessels to avoid injury.
Following the removal of the thymus specimen, the surgeon stimulates the contralateral vagus nerve to verify its integrity, ensuring no injury has occurred during the procedure.
